topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone, a familiar chime that usually signaled a new assignment or a group chat update. But this time, it was a direct mention. Professor Albright had tagged me in a post on Instagram, a picture of a sunset taken on the school grounds. My stomach tightened instantly. I scrolled through the profile, noticing the carefully curated aesthetic—perfect lighting, thoughtful captions. A slow, cold dread began to pool in my chest. I quickly minimized the app, my fingers hovering over the screen as if afraid to touch it again. The air in the classroom felt suddenly thin, the usual hum of student chatter seeming distant and hollow. I stared at my textbook, the words blurring into meaningless shapes.

[story 2]

It was late, the kind of quiet hour when the library lights cast long, lonely shadows across the tables. I was reviewing notes for tomorrow’s history exam when my phone buzzed. It was a direct message from Ms. Evans. The message was simple: a link to an article she had shared, and beneath it, a photo—a candid shot of me from a recent project presentation, taken clearly from the back row. My breath hitched. I felt a flush creep up my neck, a heat that had nothing to do with the library’s thermostat. I immediately locked my screen, shoving the device deep into my backpack. Every time I glanced toward the door, I felt a prickle along my spine, a restless energy demanding I move, to escape the silent scrutiny of that digital presence.

[story 3]

I found it accidentally while clearing out my drafts folder. A recent post from Mr. Harrison—my calculus instructor—had been cross-posted to a private community group. It was a photo of me struggling with a particularly complex problem, captioned with a wry observation about the difficulty of integration. My hands started to tremble slightly as I read the comment section, which was filled with supportive messages, yet somehow felt intensely pointed. I leaned back in my chair, gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I kept glancing at the clock, wishing desperately for the sound of the hallway, any noise that wasn't the echo of that silent, digital observation.
